core_concepts: Progressive Overload: The fundamental driver of hypertrophy; consistently increasing the stimulus over time.
Micro-loading: Using the smallest possible weight increments to ensure continuous progression without stalling.
Rep-Range Progression: Increasing reps with the same weight before increasing the load.
RPE/RIR Management: Adjusting intensity to match fatigue levels while maintaining a productive training stimulus.
Periodization (Mesocycle): Structuring training blocks to include progressive overload followed by a planned deload to manage systemic fatigue.
Technical Quality: The ceiling for progression—if form breaks down, the stimulus is no longer effective or safe.

actionable_advice.progression_methods: Prioritize increasing reps (within a target range) before increasing weight. Once the top end of the rep range is reached, increase weight by the smallest increment available (e.g., 2.5-5 lbs).
actionable_advice.tracking: Log every set, rep, and load. Without data, you cannot verify progress.
actionable_advice.frequency: Train each muscle group 2-3 times per week to maximize the number of quality 'stimulus events' per mesocycle.
actionable_advice.deload: Every 4-8 weeks, implement a deload week where volume/intensity is reduced by 30-50% to allow for supercompensation.
actionable_advice.intensity_floor: Ensure every set is taken within 1-3 Reps in Reserve (RIR) of technical failure. If you are too far from failure, the mechanical tension is likely insufficient.

progressive_overload_mechanism.description: Hypertrophy is primarily a result of increasing mechanical tension over time, necessitating progressive overload.

volume_load_relationship.description: Increasing the total volume (sets x reps x load) is a reliable proxy for hypertrophy, provided intensity is maintained.

supercompensation_cycle.description: Systemic fatigue accumulation requires planned periods of reduced training to realize growth.

coaching_insights: The most common reason for stalled progress is inconsistent effort—'coasting' on weights that were hard last week but easy today.
If you cannot add weight, adding a single high-quality rep to a set is still progress.
Consistency is the prerequisite for progression; a mediocre program followed consistently beats a perfect program followed sporadically.
Don't chase 'new' exercises to avoid plateaus; master the basics and drive up the load on them.
common_mistakes: Randomizing exercises every session, making it impossible to track true load progression.
Increasing weight too quickly, forcing form breakdown to achieve the target rep count.
Ignoring the deload phase, leading to cumulative systemic fatigue and stagnation.
Failing to track intensity (RIR), resulting in 'junk volume' sets that provide little stimulus.
Comparing progress to others rather than focusing on personal incremental gains.

motivational_quotes: Progress is the sum of small, consistent efforts.
If you aren't tracking, you're guessing.
Growth lives in the margins of your last workout.
Master the baseline before you reach for the ceiling.
Consistency isn't a long sprint; it's a series of small, steady steps.
Your last set is only as good as the data you recorded.
Hard work is easy; smart progression is the skill.
Train to improve, not just to finish.
The scale won't show it, but the logbook will.
myths_addressed: You have to change your routine to keep the muscles guessing.
Muscle confusion is the best way to grow.
If you don't feel sore, you didn't progress.
More intensity is always better than more weight.
You should never deload if you want to be big.
Tracking is only for professional bodybuilders.
You have to fail every set to make progress.
Progress is always linear (it will happen every week).
Eating more is the only way to break a plateau.
All sets are equal, regardless of tracking.

grit_application.implementation_strategy: The app should feature a 'Progression Engine'. 1. Users input their primary goal, and the app sets a target rep range (e.g., 6-10). 2. When the user logs a set, the app checks if they hit the target. If they did, it suggests a small load increase for the next session. 3. If they miss the target, it suggests staying at that weight. 4. An 'Autoregulation Toggle' allows users to log RIR, and if fatigue is high, it prevents the app from suggesting an increase to avoid injury. 5. 'Deload Prompts' appear automatically after 6 weeks of logged data.
